title: Coatings and Surface/Subsurface Discontinuities
narrationScript: Two questions decide whether magnetic particle testing will show you a discontinuity. How close is it to the surface, and what is sitting on top of the surface between the leakage field and your particles. This lesson covers both. Start with coatings. An indication forms because flux leaks out of the part where a discontinuity interrupts it, and that leakage field is strongest right at the surface and fades fast as you move away from it. A nonmagnetic coating like paint, primer, or most platings does not carry flux. It simply holds your particles farther from the leakage field, out where the field is already weaker. A thin, tight coating, on the order of a couple of thousandths of an inch, usually costs very little. As the coating gets thicker, the field available at the coating surface drops away quickly. Fine crack indications fade first, and eventually even large cracks stop showing. That is why practices such as ASTM E709 and E1444 take the position that a thin nonconductive coating may stay on the part, but beyond a small thickness the burden is on you to prove the technique still works. The usual proof is a shim type artificial flaw, the QQI, demonstrated under a nonmagnetic standoff equal to the heaviest coating you measured on the part. Measure the coating with a thickness gauge. Never guess. Coatings also matter electrically. Paint is an insulator. If your technique passes current through the part, a head shot or prods, the contact points must be stripped to bare metal or you get arcing, burning, or no current at all. A yoke is different. It puts flux into the part magnetically, with no current crossing the surface, so a yoke tolerates a thin coating that prods cannot. And watch platings. Nickel can be ferromagnetic. It carries flux itself, it can hide the leakage from a flaw underneath it, and it can crack on its own, so an indication may belong to the plating, the base metal, or both. Now the discontinuities themselves. A surface discontinuity is open to the surface. Fatigue cracks at fillets and holes. Quench cracks at section changes. Grinding cracks. Seams and laps rolled or forged into the surface of the raw material. When flux crosses one of these, the leakage field is sharp and concentrated, because the break literally interrupts the surface the particles sit on. The indication that forms is the classic one. Sharp, dense, tightly held, with well defined edges, sitting exactly on the discontinuity. Wipe it gently, re-apply particles under magnetization, and it forms again in the same place. Surface breaking cracks are what this method finds best, and the rule of orientation always applies. The flux should cross the discontinuity at something between forty five and ninety degrees, which is why complete coverage always takes two magnetizations roughly perpendicular to each other. A subsurface discontinuity lies entirely beneath the surface. Nonmetallic inclusions and stringers from the original steel making. Internal porosity. Lack of fusion buried in a weld. Internal forging bursts. The flux still has to detour around the flaw, but most of it simply crowds through the sound metal above it, and only a diluted disturbance reaches the surface. So the leakage field that does appear is spread out and weak, and the indication follows suit. Broad, fuzzy, weakly held, with soft edges. Brush it lightly and it smears or vanishes far more easily than a crack indication. How deep can you see. Be honest with yourself here, because depth is where magnetic particle quietly runs out. Under favorable conditions, direct current magnetization, dry powder, and a sizable flaw, detection to something like a quarter of an inch below the surface is commonly quoted as the practical ceiling. But sensitivity falls off rapidly with every bit of depth, and fine flaws disappear long before that. Deeper questions belong to radiography or ultrasonics. Current type is your main lever. Alternating current rides the skin of the part, the skin effect, so it concentrates the field at the surface, gives particles excellent mobility, and finds surface flaws beautifully, but it is essentially blind below the surface. Half wave rectified current pulses and penetrates, which is why half wave with dry powder is the field standard when subsurface matters, like the far side of a weld root. Full wave direct current penetrates deepest and is the bench unit workhorse. So when you read an indication, you are reading geometry. Sharp and tight means the break is at the surface. Broad and fuzzy means it is underneath, and your current type decides whether you ever had a chance to see it. Knowing which lever to pull is what turns physics into a finding you can defend.
imagePrompt: In a darkened inspection booth under deep violet UV lamps, an inspector leans over a steel weldment on a magnetic particle bench, comparing a crisp glowing green-yellow linear indication with a faint diffuse fluorescent patch nearby.

sections.heading: How a Coating Steals Sensitivity
sections.body:
An MT indication forms because leakage flux at the surface captures particles. That leakage field is strongest **right at the surface** and decays rapidly with distance above it. A nonmagnetic coating — paint, primer, powder coat, most platings — adds a standoff layer that holds the particles up where the field has already faded.

The consequences scale with thickness:

- A thin, tightly adherent coating on the order of **a couple of thousandths of an inch** usually costs little sensitivity for the flaw sizes MT is asked to find.
- As thickness grows, fine-crack indications fade first; with enough coating, even gross cracks stop showing.
- A loose, blistered, or flaking coating is unacceptable at any thickness — it produces false indications and traps bath.

Practices such as ASTM E709 and E1444 handle this with a demonstration burden: a coating beyond a small thickness may remain only if it is **demonstrated that the required sensitivity is achieved through it** — typically by showing that a shim-type artificial flaw (a **QQI**) still produces a clear indication under a nonmagnetic standoff equal to the maximum coating thickness measured on the part. Measure the coating with a thickness gauge; never assume.

sections.caption: A nonmagnetic coating holds particles at a standoff from the crack's leakage field, which decays rapidly with distance — a thin film costs little, a thick one erases the indication.

sections.heading: Coatings and the Current Path
sections.body:
Coatings also act electrically, and the effect depends on the technique.

- **Prods and head shots pass current through the part.** Paint and other nonconductive coatings are insulators: contact points must be stripped to bare metal, or the result is arcing, burning, or no current at all.
- **A yoke induces flux magnetically** — no current crosses the surface — so a yoke tolerates a thin, tight nonconductive coating that contact techniques cannot.
- **Conductive coatings** change how current enters and spreads at the contact points and must be considered in the technique.
- **Ferromagnetic platings such as nickel** are a special case: the plating carries flux itself, can mask the leakage field from a flaw beneath it, and can crack independently of the base metal — so an indication may belong to the plating, the substrate, or both.

The governing procedure decides what may stay on the part. The technician's job is to measure what is actually there and prove the technique still finds the reference flaw through it.

sections.heading: Surface Discontinuities: the Sharp Signature
sections.body:
A **surface discontinuity** is open to the examination surface. The classic populations:

- **Cracks** — fatigue cracks at fillets and bolt holes, quench cracks at section changes, grinding cracks in hardened surfaces, weld toe and crater cracks.
- **Seams** — rolled-in surface discontinuities, long and straight, running parallel to the working direction.
- **Laps** — metal folded over during forging or rolling, meeting the surface at a shallow angle.

Because the discontinuity physically breaks the surface, the flux interruption is abrupt and the leakage field is concentrated, with a steep gradient. The indication is correspondingly **sharp, dense, and tightly held**, with well-defined edges, sitting directly on the discontinuity. Wiped gently and re-applied under magnetization, it re-forms in the same place.

Orientation still rules everything: leakage forms only where flux **crosses** the discontinuity, strongest at **45 to 90 degrees**. A seam lying parallel to the flux barely disturbs it and may give no indication at all — the reason full coverage always means **two magnetizations roughly perpendicular to each other**. Laps, meeting the surface at a shallow angle, often give weaker and more irregular indications than their actual size deserves.

sections.heading: Subsurface Discontinuities: the Fuzzy Signature
sections.body:
A **subsurface discontinuity** lies wholly beneath the surface. Typical examples:

- **Nonmetallic inclusions and stringers** from steelmaking, drawn out parallel to the grain.
- **Internal porosity and gas holes** in castings and welds.
- **Buried lack of fusion or incomplete penetration** in a weld.
- **Internal bursts** from forging.

Flux must detour around a buried flaw, but most of it simply crowds through the sound metal above; only a diluted disturbance reaches the surface. The leakage field there is **broad, weak, and gently graded**, and the indication matches: **wide, fuzzy, weakly held**, with soft edges — easily wiped away and slower to re-form.

Depth is the hard limit. Indication strength falls **rapidly** as depth increases. Under favorable conditions — DC magnetization, dry powder, a sizable flaw — detection to roughly **a quarter inch** below the surface is commonly quoted as the practical ceiling, and fine discontinuities disappear at a small fraction of that. MT is a **surface and near-surface** method; truly volumetric questions belong to RT or UT.

Current type is the main lever: AC's skin effect confines the field to the surface; HWDC pulses and penetrates — the dry-powder field standard; FWDC penetrates deepest of all.

sections.caption: Surface versus subsurface discontinuities — recognition and technique selection.
rows: Fatigue, quench, and grinding cracks; seams; laps
Inclusions and stringers; porosity; buried lack of fusion; bursts
Concentrated, steep gradient at the opening
Spread out and weak by the time it reaches the surface
Sharp, dense, tightly held, well-defined edges
Broad, fuzzy, weakly held, soft edges
AC for surface concentration and particle mobility; wet fluorescent for fine cracks
HWDC or FWDC, dry powder, continuous method
Best case — the flaw meets the particles
Falls off rapidly; about a quarter inch is a practical best-case ceiling

sections.caption: The same flux meets a surface crack, a near-surface inclusion, and a deep inclusion — the surface leakage, and the indication it can hold, fades rapidly with depth.

sections.title: Read the edges
sections.body: **Sharp, dense, tightly held** — treat it as surface-breaking until proven otherwise. **Broad, fuzzy, weakly held** — suspect a subsurface source, and confirm your current type could even reach it. Either way, verify orientation coverage: a discontinuity lying nearly parallel to the flux may be showing you only a ghost of its true size, which is why complete examinations magnetize in two roughly perpendicular directions.

figures.title: Leakage field through a nonmagnetic coating

checkQuestions.question: Why does even a smooth, intact nonmagnetic coating reduce MT sensitivity?
options: It lowers the magnetic permeability of the part beneath it
It holds the particles at a standoff from the surface, where the leakage field has already decayed
It absorbs a portion of the magnetizing current
It chemically degrades fluorescent particles on contact
checkQuestions.explanation: The leakage field is strongest at the part surface and falls off rapidly with distance. A nonmagnetic coating simply lifts the particles into the weaker region — fine-flaw indications fade first as thickness grows.

checkQuestions.question: A weld is to be prod-magnetized through 8 mils of intact shop paint. The technically sound course of action is to:
options: Test as-is, because paint never affects prod techniques
Strip the contact points to bare metal, and either remove the coating from the examination area or demonstrate the required sensitivity through the measured thickness with a shim-type reference flaw
Double the amperage to push the field through the paint
Switch to the residual method, which is unaffected by coatings
checkQuestions.explanation: Prods need conductive, bare-metal contact, and 8 mils is far beyond a thin film — common practices require demonstrating detection through the measured coating thickness (typically with a QQI under an equivalent standoff) or removing the coating.

checkQuestions.question: Why can an AC yoke often examine through a thin, tight coat of paint when prods on the same part would require bare metal?
options: Yokes generate stronger fields than any current-injection technique
The yoke induces flux into the part magnetically without passing current across the surface, so an insulating film does not break the technique
The particle standoff effect does not apply to yoke fields
Paint carries flux as well as steel does, so only the current path matters
checkQuestions.explanation: A yoke magnetizes through its poles without injecting current, so an insulating coating does not interrupt the circuit. The standoff penalty on the particles still applies — the coating must remain thin — but there is no electrical contact to lose.

checkQuestions.question: On a machined shaft examined with HWDC and dry powder, an indication appears as a wide, fuzzy, weakly held band that wipes away easily and re-forms slowly. The most consistent interpretation is:
options: A tight surface fatigue crack
A subsurface discontinuity such as an inclusion or stringer below the surface
A seam open to the surface
A false indication from bath drainage
checkQuestions.explanation: Leakage from a buried flaw is diluted by the sound metal above it, so the indication is broad, fuzzy, and weakly held. Surface cracks and seams give sharp, dense, tightly held lines, and drainage patterns follow runoff paths rather than re-forming under magnetization.

checkQuestions.question: Subsurface porosity about an eighth of an inch deep is the concern on a rough casting. Which current selection is wrong for the job, and why?
options: AC — its skin effect concentrates the field at the surface, leaving almost no flux disturbance from the buried flaw
HWDC — its pulsation improves dry-powder mobility
FWDC — it gives the deepest penetration
HWDC — it pairs well with the dry continuous method
checkQuestions.explanation: AC is essentially a surface-only current because the skin effect confines the field to the outer skin of the part. HWDC and FWDC penetrate — HWDC with dry powder is the classic choice for near-surface flaws on rough work.

references: ASTM E709 and ASTM E1444/E1444M (general practice: examination through coatings, shim-standard demonstrations)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 (general practice)
